 {"id":"bd-3rwt","title":"Add wa robot workflow list command","description":"# wa robot workflow list

## Current State (CLI Stub Done)
✅ CLI subcommand structure added
✅ JSON schema created (wa-robot-workflow-list.json)
❌ Implementation uses hardcoded workflow list

## What Remains
Query WorkflowEngine registry instead of hardcoded list.

## Implementation
1. Access WorkflowEngine from command context
2. Call `engine.list_workflows()` or equivalent
3. Map to response format with name, description, triggers, parameters

## Testing Requirements
- Unit: List reflects registered workflows (not hardcoded)
- Unit: Shows enabled/disabled state correctly
- E2E: See bd-qvbz for comprehensive scenario

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Lists workflows from WorkflowEngine (not hardcoded)
- [ ] Shows enabled/disabled state
- [ ] Includes trigger event types
- [ ] JSON validates against schema
- [ ] Unit + E2E tests pass with detailed logging

-{"id":"bd-41w","title":"Connection pool for DirectMuxClient — eliminate CLI subprocess spawning","description":"## Goal
Wire the existing Pool<C> (crates/wa-core/src/pool.rs) to DirectMuxClient (crates/wa-core/src/vendored/mux_client.rs). Instead of spawning `wezterm cli` subprocesses for every operation (which creates 60+ stuck processes), reuse persistent Unix socket connections.

## Background & Motivation
The #1 operational problem with wa under agent swarm workloads is stuck CLI processes. Every wa operation (get pane text, list panes, send keys) currently spawns a `wezterm cli` subprocess, which:
1. Creates a new TCP/Unix socket connection to the mux server
2. Performs the PDU handshake (verify_codec_version, register_client)
3. Sends one request, gets one response
4. Exits

Under 50+ pane load with 200ms polling, this spawns ~250 processes/second. When the mux server is under load, these processes pile up waiting for lock contention on the Mux singleton, creating 60+ zombie processes that consume file descriptors and memory.

The Pool<C> in pool.rs already has everything we need: idle timeout eviction, semaphore-based concurrency limiting, health checks, and metrics. DirectMuxClient already speaks the raw mux protocol over Unix sockets. We just need to connect them.

## Technical Design

### DirectMuxClientPool
```rust
// Location: crates/wa-core/src/vendored/mux_pool.rs (new file)
use crate::pool::{Pool, PoolConfig, PoolItem};
use crate::vendored::mux_client::DirectMuxClient;

pub type DirectMuxClientPool = Pool<DirectMuxClient>;

impl PoolItem for DirectMuxClient {
    async fn health_check(&self) -> bool {
        // Send a lightweight ping (GetPaneList with empty filter)
        self.list_panes().await.is_ok()
    }
    
    async fn create() -> Result<Self> {
        let socket_path = find_mux_socket()?;
        let mut client = DirectMuxClient::connect(&socket_path).await?;
        client.verify_codec_version().await?;
        client.register_client().await?;
        Ok(client)
    }
}
```

### Integration with WeztermClient
```rust
// In crates/wa-core/src/wezterm.rs, replace run_cli() calls:
impl WeztermClient {
    // Before: spawns subprocess
    // pub async fn list_panes(&self) -> Result<Vec<PaneInfo>> {
    //     let output = self.run_cli(&["cli", "list", "--format", "json"]).await?;
    //     ...
    // }
    
    // After: uses pool
    pub async fn list_panes(&self) -> Result<Vec<PaneInfo>> {
        let conn = self.pool.get().await?;
        let panes = conn.list_panes().await?;
        Ok(panes)
    }
}
```

### Pool Configuration
```toml
# wa.toml
[mux_pool]
max_connections = 8          # Enough for concurrent captures
idle_timeout_seconds = 300   # Evict idle connections after 5 min
health_check_interval = 30   # Periodic health check
acquire_timeout_seconds = 10 # Max wait for a connection
```

### Existing Code References
- Pool<C>: crates/wa-core/src/pool.rs (554 lines) — has idle eviction, semaphore, metrics
- DirectMuxClient: crates/wa-core/src/vendored/mux_client.rs (997 lines) — has connect, verify_codec_version, register_client, list_panes, get_pane_text, send_text
- WeztermClient: crates/wa-core/src/wezterm.rs — run_cli() at line 895, DEFAULT_TIMEOUT_SECS at line 388

## Expected Impact
- Eliminates stuck CLI processes entirely (0 subprocess spawning for pool-supported operations)
- Reduces latency from ~400ms (subprocess spawn) to ~1ms (socket reuse)
- Reduces CPU from process spawning overhead
- Reduces file descriptor usage from 250+/sec to 8 persistent connections
- Falls back to CLI for any operation not yet supported by DirectMuxClient

## Migration Path
1. Implement DirectMuxClientPool
2. Add pool to WeztermClient struct
3. Migrate list_panes() to pool (most common, test first)
4. Migrate get_text(), send_text() one at a time
5. Keep run_cli() as fallback for unmigrated operations

## Dependencies
None — this is foundational, other beads depend on it.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Pool creates and manages DirectMuxClient connections
- Health checks detect and evict dead connections
- Concurrent operations use separate pool connections
- Idle connections evicted after timeout
- Fallback to CLI for unsupported operations
- No stuck processes under normal operation
- Metrics: pool size, acquire latency, health check failures

## Estimated Effort
4-6 hours implementation, 2 hours testing","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:17:31.915411Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-09T22:18:15.567591-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-41w","depends_on_id":"wa-3cyp","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:37:25.450214Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}

 {"id":"bd-4enj","title":"Scheduler + alert execution for saved searches","description":"## What
Add a background scheduler to execute saved searches and emit alerts.

## Why
Scheduled alerts turn wa into a proactive monitor rather than a pull-only tool.

## How
- Integrate with watcher runtime loop or dedicated task
- Enforce max frequency + backoff
- Emit notification events with redacted snippets + counts

## Risks
- Alert storm risk; must rate-limit and include dedupe window

## Success Criteria
- Scheduled searches run on interval
- Alerts include query name, scope, and match count","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"TopazStone","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:01:42.921020652Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-06T01:01:25.758826685Z","closed_at":"2026-02-06T01:01:25.758761084Z","close_reason":"done","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-4enj","depends_on_id":"bd-1t2b","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:23Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-4enj","depends_on_id":"bd-2uyz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:23Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-4enj","depends_on_id":"bd-yt9x","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:23Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"bd-4vx.10.16","title":"E2E script: RequireApproval → wa approve allow-once → send succeeds (audited)","description":"# Task: E2E script — approval allow-once

## Goal
Validate the full RequireApproval UX loop:
1) an action requires approval
2) wa returns a structured allow-once payload
3) a human grants approval via `wa approve`
4) the action succeeds on retry
5) the whole flow is auditable and redacted

This is the critical "human-in-the-loop" bridge that keeps wa safe and ergonomic.

## Scenario
- Use a policy config that forces RequireApproval for SendText in a safe situation (so this test is deterministic).
- Spawn a dummy pane that echoes received input.

Steps
1. Attempt a send (`wa send` or `wa robot send`): expect RequireApproval.
2. Extract `allow_once_code` from the output.
3. Run `wa approve <code> --yes`.
4. Retry the same send: expect Allow and successful injection.
5. Verify audit trail contains:
   - initial require-approval decision
   - approval grant
   - successful retry
   - redacted summaries (no raw secret-like input)

## Assertions
- RequireApproval returns a stable allow-once payload.
- Approval is scoped (wrong pane/action/workspace does not match).
- Retry succeeds only for the matching fingerprint.

## Artifacts
- Effective config used.
- Robot/human command outputs.
- Audit export JSONL (or query output).

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Script is deterministic.
- On failure, artifacts clearly distinguish:
  - policy decision bug
  - approval storage/lookup bug
  - audit/redaction bug


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add a negative assertion: use the allow-once code against a different pane/workspace and ensure it is rejected.
  - If approvals have TTL, assert that an expired token is rejected (or explicitly document if TTL is not implemented).

- Artifact validation:
  - Assert audit artifacts include three distinct records (require-approval, approve, allowed retry) and are fully redacted.

 {"id":"bd-4vx.10.17","title":"E2E script: pane exclude filter prevents capture (ignored pane absent from search, shown in status)","description":"# Task: E2E script — pane exclude filter

## Goal
Validate that pane selection filters protect privacy and behave predictably:
- excluded panes are visible (as ignored) but not ingested
- excluded panes do not appear in search results

## Scenario
- Spawn two dummy panes:
  1) "observed" pane prints OBSERVED_TOKEN
  2) "ignored" pane prints SECRET_TOKEN and has a title/cwd that matches an exclude rule

- Configure wa with an exclude rule that matches the ignored pane.

Steps
1) Start wa watch with the configured workspace.
2) Wait until the observed token is persisted (via wa robot search).
3) Assert that searching for SECRET_TOKEN returns no results.
4) Assert wa status / wa robot state shows the ignored pane as ignored with a reason.

## Assertions
- Observed pane is captured and searchable.
- Ignored pane is not captured and not searchable.
- Status output makes the ignore decision obvious.

## Artifacts
- Effective config used (including pane rules).
- wa watch logs with decision messages (no secrets).
- wa robot state JSON.
- wa robot search JSON for both tokens.

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test is deterministic.
- On failure, artifacts show whether the bug is:
  - filter matching
  - ingest mistakenly tailing ignored panes
  - search indexing
  - status/state reporting


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Assert that SECRET_TOKEN never appears in artifacts/logs (privacy guarantee), not just “not searchable”.
  - Add a control assertion that OBSERVED_TOKEN does appear in search to avoid false negatives.

- Artifact validation:
  - Assert state/status artifacts include the ignore reason so the user can understand why capture was skipped.
